Functional capacity <4 METs (cannot climb one flight of stairs) is correctly characterized as a marker of INCREASED risk, and the recommended next step (further noninvasive testing only if it would change management; otherwise optimize and proceed) matches ACC/AHA guidance. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: zero-risk (false, meets 4), poor capacity is reassuring (backwards), only surgery counts (false, comorbidities count), and absolute permanent contraindication (overstated). The explanation accurately enumerates all six RCRI components. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK493196/ resolved to "Perioperative Cardiac Management - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf," which explicitly covers the RCRI/Lee index, all six criteria (including creatinine >2 mg/dL and insulin therapy), METs-based functional capacity, and perioperative risk stratification — fully on-topic and matching the cited title.

stem: During the preoperative interview for an elective knee arthroscopy, a healthy 19-year-old man reports that his older brother 'had a dangerous reaction to anesthesia, ran a very high fever and his muscles got stiff,' and that a maternal uncle died unexpectedly during a routine operation. The patient has never had surgery himself. Which preoperative decision most appropriately addresses this history?

choices.text: Plan a nontriggering anesthetic that avoids volatile halogenated agents and succinylcholine, and ensure dantrolene and a malignant hyperthermia plan are available.
choices.rationale: Correct: a credible family history of a malignant hyperthermia-like event makes this patient potentially susceptible, so a trigger-free technique (e.g., total intravenous anesthesia) with dantrolene immediately available is the safest plan.

choices.text: Proceed with a standard sevoflurane-based general anesthetic, since the patient himself has never reacted to anesthesia.
choices.rationale: Malignant hyperthermia susceptibility is inherited and frequently unmasked at first exposure, so a lack of personal anesthetic history does not make volatile agents safe here.

choices.text: Administer succinylcholine to test for masseter rigidity before committing to a full anesthetic.
choices.rationale: Deliberately giving a known triggering agent to 'test' a susceptible patient is dangerous and can precipitate a fulminant crisis rather than safely screen for it.

choices.text: Reassure the family that this reaction is unrelated to today's anesthetic and requires no change in plan.
choices.rationale: The described fever and muscle rigidity strongly suggest malignant hyperthermia, which is heritable and directly relevant, so dismissing it is unsafe.

choices.text: Cancel the case indefinitely because no safe anesthetic option exists for potentially susceptible patients.
choices.rationale: Potentially susceptible patients can be anesthetized safely with a nontriggering technique, so indefinite cancellation is unnecessary.

explanation: Malignant hyperthermia is an autosomal-dominant pharmacogenetic disorder, most often linked to RYR1 variants, triggered by volatile halogenated anesthetics and succinylcholine. A family history of a hypermetabolic reaction (hyperthermia, muscle rigidity) or unexplained perioperative death should make a patient be treated as potentially susceptible until proven otherwise. The appropriate plan is a trigger-free anesthetic (such as total intravenous anesthesia) on a clean machine, with dantrolene and a malignant hyperthermia treatment protocol immediately available; deliberately administering a trigger to 'test' the patient is contraindicated.
subtopic: Preoperative history and malignant hyperthermia susceptibility

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem (family history of a hypermetabolic reaction with hyperthermia + muscle rigidity in a sibling, plus an unexplained perioperative death in a maternal relative) is a textbook MH-susceptibility presentation. The keyed answer (Choice 0: nontriggering anesthetic avoiding volatile halogenated agents and succinylcholine, with dantrolene and an MH plan available) is the single best, guideline-concordant management. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: proceeding with sevoflurane ignores that MH is inherited and frequently unmasked at first exposure; deliberately giving succinylcholine to 'test' administers a known trigger and can precipitate a crisis; reassuring/dismissing ignores a heritable relevant risk; indefinite cancellation is unnecessary since trigger-free technique is safe. The explanation is accurate and current (autosomal-dominant, RYR1-linked pharmacogenetic disorder; triggers = volatile halogenated anesthetics + succinylcholine; treat credible family history as potentially susceptible; TIVA on a clean machine with dantrolene available). REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430828/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Malignant Hyperthermia' article, which is directly on-topic and corroborates every clinical claim (autosomal dominant, RYR1 primary mutation, halogenated gas/succinylcholine triggers, family history precautions, dantrolene dosing, nontriggering IV anesthetic). No reference mismatch, no 404. Both dimensions sound.

stem: A 34-year-old man with a complete spinal cord injury at C6 (8 months prior) presents for cystoscopy and lithotripsy of a bladder stone. A spinal anesthetic is declined and general anesthesia with an LMA is chosen. Shortly after the surgeon begins bladder distension, his blood pressure rises abruptly from 110/70 to 220/115 mmHg and his heart rate falls from 78 to 46/min. He develops flushing and profuse sweating above the clavicles with pale, cool, piloerect skin over the legs. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?

choices.text: Ask the surgeon to stop bladder distension and drain the bladder while deepening anesthesia
choices.rationale: Correct: removing the noxious stimulus below the lesion is the first-line intervention for autonomic dysreflexia, and deepening anesthesia raises the threshold for the reflex sympathetic surge.

choices.text: Administer atropine 0.5 mg IV to treat the bradycardia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the bradycardia is a baroreceptor-mediated vagal response to severe hypertension, so treating the heart rate without addressing the hypertension is misdirected and could worsen the picture.

choices.text: Give a 500 mL crystalloid bolus and place the patient in Trendelenburg
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the problem is a hypertensive crisis from unopposed sympathetic outflow, not hypovolemia, so fluid loading and head-down positioning are inappropriate and may raise pressure further.

choices.text: Start a phenylephrine infusion to support perfusion pressure
choices.rationale: Incorrect: phenylephrine is a vasoconstrictor that would dangerously exacerbate the existing severe hypertension.

choices.text: Administer IV dexmedetomidine as the definitive treatment before any other action
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while sympatholytic agents can help, the immediate priority is stimulus removal, and reaching first for an infusion delays the single most effective maneuver.

explanation: Autonomic dysreflexia occurs in patients with spinal cord injury at or above T6 when a noxious stimulus below the lesion (most commonly bladder or bowel distension) triggers a massive, unopposed sympathetic discharge that descending inhibitory pathways can no longer modulate. The hallmark is paroxysmal hypertension with reflex (baroreceptor-mediated) bradycardia, flushing and sweating above the lesion, and pallor/piloerection below it. First-line management is immediate removal of the inciting stimulus and deepening anesthesia; refractory hypertension is then treated with rapid-onset, short-acting vasodilators (e.g., nitroprusside, nitroglycerin, or nifedipine).
subtopic: Autonomic dysreflexia in chronic spinal cord injury

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Classic autonomic dysreflexia in chronic (8-mo) complete C6 SCI (above T6) triggered by bladder distension during cystoscopy/lithotripsy. Presentation is internally consistent and textbook: paroxysmal HTN (110/70->220/115), baroreceptor-mediated reflex bradycardia (78->46), flushing/sweating above the lesion, pallor/piloerection below. Keyed answer A (stop distension + drain bladder + deepen anesthesia = remove the noxious stimulus below the lesion) is the universally accepted single best first-line maneuver. All distractors are genuinely inferior: atropine treats a reflex bradycardia in isolation and is misdirected; crystalloid+Trendelenburg misframes a hypertensive crisis as hypovolemia and head-down worsens it (positioning should be head-up); phenylephrine is a vasoconstrictor that would exacerbate severe HTN; dexmedetomidine 'before any other action' wrongly delays stimulus removal. Explanation (T6 threshold, loss of descending inhibition/unopposed sympathetic outflow, short-acting vasodilators nitroprusside/NTG/nifedipine as second-line) is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482434/ resolved to the StatPearls 'Autonomic Dysreflexia' article, directly on-topic, confirming T6-and-above threshold, ~85% bladder triggers, the above/below-lesion presentation, and stimulus removal + vasodilator management.

stem: A 58-year-old woman undergoes resection of an acoustic neuroma in the sitting position under general anesthesia with controlled ventilation. End-tidal CO2 is 34 mmHg and stable. During tumor dissection, the precordial Doppler abruptly changes to a loud churning 'mill-wheel' character, the end-tidal CO2 falls acutely to 19 mmHg, and the systemic blood pressure drops to 76/40 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Increase the inspired oxygen concentration to 100% and continue dissection
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while 100% oxygen is appropriate, simply continuing the operation fails to stop ongoing air entrainment, which is the key driver of the deterioration.

choices.text: Notify the surgeon to flood the field with saline and apply bone wax to open venous channels
choices.rationale: Correct: alerting the surgeon to occlude the open venous sinuses (flooding the field, packing, bone wax) stops further air entrainment and is the central intervention in suspected venous air embolism.

choices.text: Apply 10 cm H2O of PEEP to raise central venous pressure and prevent further entrainment
choices.rationale: Incorrect: PEEP is of unproven benefit, can reduce cardiac output, and may paradoxically promote right-to-left (paradoxical) air embolism, so it is not the immediate priority.

choices.text: Administer a beta-blocker to treat the associated tachycardia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the patient is hypotensive from acute right heart obstruction, and negative inotropy/chronotropy from a beta-blocker would worsen cardiovascular collapse.

choices.text: Disconnect the breathing circuit to allow the lungs to deflate and the air to escape
choices.rationale: Incorrect: disconnecting the circuit removes oxygenation and ventilation without addressing the air entry point and does nothing to relieve the air lock.

explanation: In the sitting position, the operative site lies above the heart, so an open, non-collapsible venous channel (such as a dural sinus or diploic vein) can entrain air down a pressure gradient, producing venous air embolism. Classic signs include a sudden fall in end-tidal CO2 (from increased dead space), a 'mill-wheel' murmur or change in precordial Doppler tones, and hypotension from air-lock obstruction of right ventricular outflow. Management is to immediately stop further entrainment: notify the surgeon to flood/pack the field, give 100% oxygen, attempt aspiration from a right atrial catheter, provide hemodynamic support, and consider repositioning to the left lateral head-down position.
subtopic: Venous air embolism during sitting-position neurosurgery

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem is a classic venous air embolism (VAE) presentation in sitting-position neurosurgery (mill-wheel murmur on precordial Doppler, acute ETCO2 drop 34->19, hypotension from RV outflow air-lock). The keyed answer (B: notify surgeon to flood the field and apply bone wax) is the single best next step because the priority is to stop ongoing air entrainment at the source. All distractors are genuinely inferior: A pairs correct 100% O2 with "continue dissection," which fails to stop entrainment; C (PEEP) is unproven, lowers CO, and risks paradoxical embolism; D (beta-blocker) is harmful in a hypotensive patient; E (circuit disconnect) abandons ventilation without addressing the source. Rationales and explanation (right atrial catheter aspiration, left lateral head-down repositioning, dead-space/V-Q mechanism for ETCO2 fall) are accurate and current. Minor note: distractor D's rationale references "associated tachycardia" not stated in the stem, but this is internal to the distractor and does not create answer ambiguity. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/venous_air_embolism/ resolves (HTTP 200) and is directly on-topic, explicitly confirming the mill-wheel murmur, ETCO2 fall, surgeon flooding field/bone wax, 100% O2, right-heart catheter aspiration, and the non-collapsible venous sinus risk in sitting craniotomy. Both clinical and reference are sound; keep.

stem: A 24-year-old man with a severe traumatic brain injury is intubated and mechanically ventilated in the neuro-ICU. An intracranial pressure monitor reads 22 mmHg and mean arterial pressure is 70 mmHg. A junior trainee, aiming to 'protect the brain,' deliberately hyperventilates the patient to an arterial PaCO2 of 25 mmHg. Compared with maintaining normocapnia, this intervention will most likely have which effect on cerebral physiology in the acutely injured brain?

choices.text: Increase cerebral blood flow by impairing pressure autoregulation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: hypocapnia causes cerebral vasoconstriction and reduces cerebral blood flow rather than increasing it.

choices.text: Decrease cerebral blood flow and risk worsening regional ischemia
choices.rationale: Correct: hypocapnia produces cerebral arteriolar vasoconstriction that lowers cerebral blood flow, which in an already injured brain with critically reduced perfusion can precipitate ischemia despite the modest fall in intracranial pressure.

choices.text: Have no meaningful effect on cerebral blood flow because autoregulation keeps it constant
choices.rationale: Incorrect: cerebral blood flow is highly sensitive to PaCO2 across a wide range, and CO2 reactivity is largely independent of the pressure-autoregulation mechanism.

choices.text: Shift the autoregulation plateau rightward so a higher MAP is needed
choices.rationale: Incorrect: this describes the effect of chronic hypertension or sympathetic activation on the autoregulatory curve, not the CO2-mediated vascular response to acute hyperventilation.

choices.text: Increase cerebral blood flow via cerebral vasodilation from respiratory alkalosis
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the alkalosis from hypocapnia causes vasoconstriction, not vasodilation, so this reverses the actual direction of the effect.

explanation: Cerebral blood flow varies roughly linearly with PaCO2 over the range of about 20-80 mmHg, with each 1 mmHg change altering CBF by approximately 1-2 mL/100 g/min; hypocapnia causes vasoconstriction and reduced CBF. Although acute hyperventilation transiently lowers intracranial pressure by reducing cerebral blood volume, in traumatic brain injury aggressive prophylactic hyperventilation to a PaCO2 of about 25 mmHg can drop CBF below the ischemic threshold in already hypoperfused tissue. Current guidance reserves brief, moderate hyperventilation for impending herniation rather than routine ICP control.
subtopic: Cerebral blood flow physiology and CO2 reactivity in TBI

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Stem (TBI, ICP 22, MAP 70 giving low CPP ~48, prophylactic hyperventilation to PaCO2 25) is realistic. Keyed answer B (decrease CBF, risk regional ischemia) is the single best answer: hypocapnia causes respiratory alkalosis -> cerebral arteriolar vasoconstriction -> reduced CBF, which in already-hypoperfused injured brain can cross the ischemic threshold. This matches Brain Trauma Foundation guidance against prophylactic hyperventilation to PaCO2 <=25, reserving brief moderate hyperventilation for impending herniation. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: A and E invert the direction (hypocapnia constricts, not dilates/increases flow), C wrongly claims pressure autoregulation holds CBF constant despite strong CO2 reactivity, and D describes the chronic-hypertension/sympathetic rightward shift rather than the acute CO2 response. Each rationale is accurate. The only trivial nuance is the explanation citing a 20-80 mmHg linear range vs the reference's 25-70 mmHg, a benign cross-source variation that does not affect the key. REFERENCE: WebFetched the OpenAnesthesia 'Cerebral Autoregulation' page; it resolves (not 404) and is directly on-topic, explicitly stating ~1-2 mL/100g/min per mmHg PaCO2 CO2 reactivity and that prolonged hyperventilation should be avoided due to ischemia risk. Both clinical and reference are sound -> keep.

stem: A 33-year-old G1 at 38 weeks' gestation with severe preeclampsia is admitted for induction of labor. She is started on an intravenous magnesium sulfate infusion for seizure prophylaxis, plus labetalol for blood pressure control. Several hours later she becomes drowsy and her deep tendon reflexes are absent on examination. Her respiratory rate is 9/min, blood pressure 150/95 mmHg, and the fetal heart tracing remains reassuring. A serum magnesium level is pending. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

choices.text: Switch the magnesium infusion to a benzodiazepine for seizure prophylaxis
choices.rationale: Incorrect: benzodiazepines do not reverse magnesium toxicity and add to her respiratory depression and sedation.

choices.text: Stop the magnesium infusion and administer intravenous calcium gluconate
choices.rationale: Correct: loss of deep tendon reflexes and respiratory depression indicate magnesium toxicity, so the infusion must be stopped and IV calcium given to directly antagonize magnesium at the neuromuscular junction.

choices.text: Continue magnesium and add intravenous furosemide to enhance renal excretion
choices.rationale: Incorrect: continuing magnesium in the setting of toxicity is dangerous, and diuresis is too slow to address impending respiratory arrest.

choices.text: Administer naloxone to reverse the depressed level of consciousness
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the sedation and hyporeflexia are from magnesium, not opioids, so naloxone has no role here.

choices.text: Increase the labetalol dose to treat the elevated blood pressure first
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the immediate life threat is neuromuscular and respiratory depression from magnesium, not the moderately elevated blood pressure.

explanation: Magnesium sulfate has a narrow therapeutic window: therapeutic levels are roughly 4-7 mEq/L, loss of deep tendon reflexes occurs around 8-10 mEq/L, and respiratory depression and cardiac arrest follow at higher levels. The first clinical sign of toxicity is loss of patellar reflexes, making it a key monitored endpoint. Management is to stop the infusion and give IV calcium gluconate (or calcium chloride), which physiologically antagonizes magnesium at the neuromuscular junction; supportive ventilation is added as needed.
subtopic: Magnesium sulfate toxicity in the preeclamptic parturient

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The stem is a classic magnesium sulfate toxicity presentation in a preeclamptic parturient (drowsiness, absent DTRs, RR 9/min) and the keyed answer (B: stop Mg + IV calcium gluconate) is the unambiguous single best answer. Calcium physiologically antagonizes Mg at the neuromuscular junction — textbook management. All distractors are genuinely inferior: benzodiazepine (A) adds respiratory depression and does not reverse Mg; continuing Mg + furosemide (C) is dangerous and diuresis too slow; naloxone (D) is useless since sedation is not opioid-mediated; increasing labetalol (E) ignores the actual life threat and the BP is only moderately elevated. Toxicity thresholds in the explanation (therapeutic ~4-7 mEq/L, loss of DTRs ~8-10 mEq/L, respiratory depression at higher levels) are standard and internally consistent; they align with the StatPearls table (patellar reflexes lost at 4-5 mmol/L = 8-10 mEq/L). REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554553/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Magnesium Sulfate' article, which explicitly covers toxicity thresholds (loss of patellar reflexes, respiratory depression, cardiac arrest) and management (discontinue Mg immediately, give parenteral calcium gluconate). Citation is accurate and on-topic. No clinical or reference issues.

stem: A 28-year-old nonsmoking woman with a history of motion sickness and prior postoperative nausea presents for outpatient laparoscopic cholecystectomy under general anesthesia. The surgeon expects to use postoperative oral oxycodone for pain. Using the Apfel simplified score to stratify her risk, which perioperative anesthetic plan best reduces her likelihood of postoperative nausea and vomiting and supports same-day discharge?

choices.text: Total intravenous anesthesia with propofol, avoidance of nitrous oxide, and combination prophylaxis with dexamethasone at induction plus ondansetron near the end of surgery
choices.rationale: Correct: she has all four Apfel risk factors (female, nonsmoker, history of PONV/motion sickness, anticipated postoperative opioids), so a multimodal strategy combining a propofol-based technique, avoidance of emetogenic agents, and two antiemetics acting at different receptors gives the greatest risk reduction.

choices.text: Volatile maintenance with sevoflurane plus nitrous oxide, with ondansetron given only if she vomits in recovery
choices.rationale: Incorrect: volatile agents and nitrous oxide are emetogenic and rescue-only dosing ignores her high baseline risk, since prophylaxis is more effective than treating established PONV.

choices.text: A single dose of ondansetron at induction as monotherapy, with no change to the volatile-based anesthetic
choices.rationale: Incorrect: single-agent prophylaxis is inadequate for a patient with three or four risk factors, who benefits from two or more interventions acting on different pathways.

choices.text: Dexamethasone given in the post-anesthesia care unit at the first sign of nausea, with sevoflurane maintenance
choices.rationale: Incorrect: dexamethasone has a slow onset and is most effective when given prophylactically at induction, not as rescue, and pairing it with a volatile technique leaves the emetogenic stimulus unaddressed.

choices.text: Metoclopramide 10 mg at the end of surgery as the sole antiemetic, with nitrous oxide maintained throughout
choices.rationale: Incorrect: metoclopramide at standard 10 mg doses has weak antiemetic efficacy and is insufficient monotherapy for a high-risk patient, especially when nitrous oxide is continued.

explanation: The Apfel simplified score counts four factors — female sex, nonsmoking status, history of PONV or motion sickness, and anticipated postoperative opioids — with predicted PONV risk rising to roughly 80% when all four are present. For high-risk patients, guidelines favor combining a propofol-based (TIVA) technique, avoidance of nitrous oxide and volatile agents, and at least two prophylactic antiemetics that act on different receptors, such as dexamethasone (given at induction for its slow onset) and a 5-HT3 antagonist like ondansetron (given near the end of surgery). Prevention with multimodal prophylaxis is more effective than treating established PONV and is critical to avoid unplanned admission after ambulatory surgery.
subtopic: PONV risk stratification and multimodal prophylaxis in the ambulatory patient

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The patient has all four Apfel simplified risk factors (female, nonsmoker, history of PONV/motion sickness counted as one factor, anticipated postoperative opioids) yielding ~80% predicted risk; the keyed claim of all four factors holds because the other three are independently present. Answer A (propofol TIVA, avoid nitrous oxide, dexamethasone at induction + ondansetron near end of surgery — two agents on different receptors) is the guideline-concordant best strategy for a high-risk patient and is the single best answer. All distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: B/D leave emetogenic volatile/N2O stimulus and use rescue-only dosing; C is inadequate monotherapy for 3–4 factors; E correctly notes metoclopramide 10 mg has weak efficacy as sole agent. Timing details (dexamethasone at induction for slow onset, ondansetron near end of surgery for short half-life) are current and correct. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK500029/ resolves to StatPearls 'Postoperative Nausea,' which explicitly covers the Apfel risk factors (nonsmoking females with PONV/motion sickness history, plus volatiles, N2O, postop opioids), states prevention is more effective than treatment, and endorses propofol/TIVA, dexamethasone, and ondansetron — directly on-topic and supportive of the keyed answer.

stem: A healthy 35-year-old man is scheduled for an outpatient knee arthroscopy in the lithotomy-adjacent position under spinal anesthesia, with the surgeon and patient both wanting a rapid recovery for same-day discharge. The anesthesia care team is choosing a local anesthetic for the subarachnoid block. Which choice most appropriately balances block duration against the risk of a recovery-delaying complication in this ambulatory setting?

choices.text: Avoid hyperbaric 5% lidocaine because of its disproportionately high incidence of transient neurologic symptoms, and select an agent such as low-dose bupivacaine or a short-acting alternative instead
choices.rationale: Correct: intrathecal lidocaine carries a markedly higher rate of transient neurologic symptoms than bupivacaine or tetracaine, and this risk is amplified by ambulatory status and lithotomy positioning, making it a poor choice despite its short duration.

choices.text: Choose hyperbaric 5% lidocaine specifically because its association with transient neurologic symptoms makes it the safest neuraxial agent for outpatients
choices.rationale: Incorrect: this reverses the evidence — lidocaine is associated with a higher, not lower, incidence of transient neurologic symptoms, and these symptoms can delay or complicate discharge.

choices.text: Choose standard high-dose 0.75% bupivacaine to guarantee the longest possible motor block
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a long, dense motor and sympathetic block delays ambulation, voiding, and discharge, which is counterproductive for a same-day case where rapid recovery is the goal.

choices.text: Use intrathecal tetracaine at a high dose, since it has the fastest offset of all spinal local anesthetics
choices.rationale: Incorrect: tetracaine is long-acting with a slow offset, so a high dose would prolong recovery and is not the fastest-offset agent.

choices.text: Substitute a peripheral nerve block is unnecessary; instead give intrathecal lidocaine but pretreat with a benzodiazepine to prevent transient neurologic symptoms
choices.rationale: Incorrect: benzodiazepines do not prevent transient neurologic symptoms, which are related to the local anesthetic itself, so this does not mitigate the lidocaine-specific risk.

explanation: Transient neurologic symptoms — back and buttock pain radiating to the legs after resolution of the block — occur much more frequently with intrathecal lidocaine (relative risk roughly 5 versus bupivacaine), and lithotomy positioning, outpatient status, and obesity further increase the risk. For ambulatory spinal anesthesia, the agent should provide a block just long enough for the procedure while minimizing recovery-delaying complications, so low-dose bupivacaine or another short-acting alternative is generally preferred over 5% lidocaine. The anesthetist must weigh duration against the side-effect profile and the goal of timely, uncomplicated same-day discharge.
subtopic: Neuraxial local anesthetic selection for ambulatory surgery (transient neurologic symptoms)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 0) is the single best answer and is medically correct. Intrathecal hyperbaric 5% lidocaine has a markedly higher incidence of transient neurologic symptoms (TNS) than bupivacaine or tetracaine (Cochrane/Zaric data: RR roughly 4-7), and lithotomy positioning and outpatient/ambulatory status are established risk multipliers — so favoring low-dose bupivacaine or a short-acting alternative (e.g., 2-chloroprocaine) is correct ambulatory practice. All four distractors are genuinely and clearly inferior: (1) reverses the evidence claiming lidocaine is "safest"; (2) high-dose 0.75% bupivacaine needlessly prolongs motor/sympathetic block and delays discharge; (3) falsely calls tetracaine the fastest-offset agent when it is long-acting; (4) benzodiazepine pretreatment does not prevent TNS, which is a local-anesthetic-intrinsic phenomenon. The explanation (RR ~5 vs bupivacaine; lithotomy/outpatient/obesity as risk factors) is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537299/ resolves to the StatPearls "Spinal Anesthesia" article (Olawin & Das), which lists the relevant agents (lidocaine 5%, bupivacaine 0.75%, tetracaine) and explicitly notes TNS "especially with lidocaine" — genuinely on-topic and supportive of the answer. Minor non-disqualifying nit: choice 4's text is grammatically garbled ("Substitute a peripheral nerve block is unnecessary; instead give..."), but this does not create answer ambiguity or alter the unambiguous correct key.

stem: A 68-year-old man (90 kg, ASA III) undergoes spinal anesthesia for a right total knee arthroplasty. A thigh tourniquet is inflated to 300 mmHg. The case is prolonged and the tourniquet has now been inflated for 135 minutes when the surgeon releases it to assess hemostasis. Over the next 2 minutes, the patient is mechanically ventilated with unchanged settings. Which set of changes is MOST likely to occur immediately after deflation?

choices.text: Rise in end-tidal CO2, transient hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, and a fall in arterial blood pressure
choices.rationale: Correct: deflation washes out the ischemic limb's accumulated CO2, potassium, lactate, and hydrogen ions while central blood volume shifts back into the reperfused leg, producing exactly this constellation.

choices.text: Fall in end-tidal CO2, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, and a rise in arterial blood pressure
choices.rationale: Incorrect: this is the opposite of reperfusion physiology, which delivers acidic, CO2- and potassium-rich blood centrally rather than removing it.

choices.text: Rise in end-tidal CO2 with a sustained increase in systemic vascular resistance and hypertension
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the EtCO2 rise is real, but deflation lowers central volume and resistance transiently, causing hypotension rather than hypertension.

choices.text: No change in end-tidal CO2 with an isolated rise in core temperature
choices.rationale: Incorrect: EtCO2 reliably peaks about 1 minute after release, and reperfusion does not produce an acute isolated temperature spike.

choices.text: Fall in end-tidal CO2 with profound hypertension from catecholamine surge
choices.rationale: Incorrect: EtCO2 rises rather than falls, and the dominant hemodynamic effect of deflation is hypotension, not a hypertensive catecholamine surge.

explanation: Prolonged tourniquet inflation renders the limb ischemic, accumulating CO2, lactate, hydrogen ions, and potassium. On deflation these byproducts wash into the central circulation, producing a transient rise in end-tidal CO2 (peaking ~1 minute, normalizing by ~10 minutes), mild hyperkalemia, and metabolic acidosis. Simultaneously, central blood volume redistributes into the reperfused limb, lowering CVP and arterial pressure for roughly 15 minutes. Anticipating these changes allows the team to increase minute ventilation and support blood pressure around release.
subtopic: Orthopedics — pneumatic tourniquet deflation physiology

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 0 — rise in EtCO2, transient hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, fall in arterial BP) is the single, unambiguous best answer and represents textbook tourniquet deflation/reperfusion physiology. With unchanged ventilator settings, washout of accumulated CO2 raises EtCO2, washout of K+ produces transient hyperkalemia, washout of lactate/H+ causes metabolic acidosis, and central volume redistribution into the reperfused limb lowers CVP/arterial BP. All four components co-occur and are internally consistent. Every distractor contains at least one clearly false element (reversed acid-base/electrolyte directions, claimed sustained increased SVR/hypertension, 'no change' or 'fall' in EtCO2, or a hypertensive catecholamine surge), so all are genuinely inferior. Rationales and the explanation (EtCO2 peaking ~1 min, normalizing ~10 min; ~15-min BP reduction) are accurate and current. The phrase 'redistributes into the reperfused limb' is correct and not contradictory. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/perioperative-tourniquet-use/ resolves with full content and is directly on-topic, explicitly confirming EtCO2 peak at 1 min/return by 10 min, K+ rise ~0.28 mmol/L after 60-120 min, temporary metabolic acidosis, and a 15-min reduction in CVP/arterial BP. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 74-year-old man undergoes monopolar transurethral resection of the prostate under spinal anesthesia for benign prostatic hyperplasia, with 1.5% glycine as the irrigating solution. About 50 minutes into a difficult resection with several opened venous sinuses, he becomes restless and nauseated, complains of blurred vision, and reports a headache. Heart rate is 52/min and blood pressure has risen to 178/96 mmHg. A stat sodium returns at 116 mEq/L. Which intervention is the MOST appropriate FIRST step?

choices.text: Administer a 1 L bolus of lactated Ringer's solution to restore intravascular volume
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the patient is volume-overloaded from absorbed hypotonic irrigant, so additional crystalloid worsens dilutional hyponatremia and pulmonary congestion.

choices.text: Communicate with the surgeon to terminate the resection and stop the irrigation as soon as feasible
choices.rationale: Correct: halting ongoing absorption of hypotonic glycine irrigant is the essential first maneuver, after which oxygenation, diuresis, and sodium correction can proceed.

choices.text: Give atropine 0.5 mg IV to treat the bradycardia before doing anything else
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the bradycardia and hypertension reflect fluid overload and rising intracranial pressure from TURP syndrome, not a primary vagal problem requiring atropine first.

choices.text: Rapidly infuse 250 mL of 3% hypertonic saline over 10 minutes before any other action
choices.rationale: Incorrect: hypertonic saline may be needed for severe symptoms, but giving it before stopping the source of absorption and confirming the trajectory risks over-rapid correction; the irrigant must be stopped first.

choices.text: Convert the spinal to general anesthesia with endotracheal intubation to control the airway
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the awake neuraxial patient's symptoms are what alerted you to the syndrome, and reflexively deepening anesthesia delays addressing the underlying ongoing fluid absorption.

explanation: TURP syndrome is symptomatic dilutional hyponatremia and volume overload from systemic absorption of hypotonic irrigating fluid through opened prostatic venous sinuses. Early neurologic and cardiovascular signs (restlessness, visual disturbance, headache, bradycardia with hypertension) are more readily detected under neuraxial than general anesthesia. The first priority is to stop the ongoing absorption by ending the resection and stopping irrigation; subsequent management includes oxygenation, fluid restriction, a loop diuretic, and 3% saline reserved for severe symptomatic hyponatremia, correcting sodium no faster than about 10 mEq/L per 24 hours.
subtopic: Urology — TURP syndrome (dilutional hyponatremia)
reference_title: Hyponatermia and Transurethral Resection of Prostate Syndrome - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Stem is a textbook monopolar TURP syndrome presentation (glycine 1.5% irrigant, opened venous sinuses, ~50 min resection, neuro signs + bradycardia/hypertension from volume overload, Na 116). The keyed answer — terminate resection and stop irrigation — is the universally accepted FIRST step because halting ongoing hypotonic-fluid absorption must precede all downstream therapy. Distractors are all genuinely inferior: LR bolus worsens dilutional overload; atropine targets a non-primary bradycardia (HR 52 needs no urgent treatment); 3% saline 'before any other action' is a treat-the-number trap that ignores stopping the source and over-rapid-correction risk; converting to GA delays the real problem and removes the awake exam that detected it. The explanation's correction-rate target (~10 mEq/L per 24 h) is current and accurate. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves and is directly on-topic; it states verbatim that the surgeon should stop irrigation, discontinue resection, and terminate the procedure as the initial step, confirming the key.

stem: A 41-year-old woman undergoes operative hysteroscopy under general anesthesia for resection of a large submucosal fibroid, using 1.5% glycine as the distension/irrigation medium. Ninety minutes in, the estimated fluid deficit (inflow minus outflow) reaches 2,400 mL. She develops hypertension and bradycardia, and after emergence she is somnolent and intermittently confused with no focal deficit. Serum sodium is 119 mEq/L. Given the specific irrigant used, which additional laboratory derangement is MOST likely contributing to her encephalopathy beyond the hyponatremia alone?

choices.text: Hyperammonemia from hepatic metabolism of absorbed glycine
choices.rationale: Correct: glycine is metabolized in the liver to ammonia, so large-volume glycine absorption produces hyperammonemia that compounds the cerebral effects of dilutional hyponatremia.

choices.text: Hyperkalemia from cellular potassium shift
choices.rationale: Incorrect: glycine absorption and dilutional hyponatremia do not characteristically raise potassium, and hyperkalemia would not explain a glycine-specific encephalopathy.

choices.text: Severe hypoglycemia from glycine-stimulated insulin release
choices.rationale: Incorrect: glycine is not a clinically significant insulin secretagogue in this setting and hypoglycemia is not a recognized feature of glycine absorption syndrome.

choices.text: Hypercalcemia from impaired renal calcium excretion
choices.rationale: Incorrect: calcium derangement is not a feature of glycine irrigant absorption and does not explain the acute encephalopathy.

choices.text: Methemoglobinemia from a glycine metabolite
choices.rationale: Incorrect: glycine metabolism produces ammonia and glycolic/glyoxylic acid, not methemoglobin, so this would not appear on routine labs as the culprit.

explanation: Operative hysteroscopy carries the same risk of hypotonic fluid absorption as TURP, since opened venous sinuses and intrauterine distension pressure drive systemic uptake; a fluid deficit above ~1,000–1,500 mL with hypotonic glycine should prompt concern. When glycine is the medium, hepatic metabolism generates ammonia, so absorbed glycine causes hyperammonemia in addition to dilutional hyponatremia, and both contribute to the visual disturbance, somnolence, and encephalopathy seen clinically. Management mirrors TURP syndrome: stop the procedure and absorption, support oxygenation, restrict free water, use a loop diuretic, and reserve hypertonic saline for severe symptomatic hyponatremia while correcting sodium slowly.
subtopic: GYN — operative hysteroscopy hypotonic fluid absorption (glycine/hyperammonemia)
reference_title: Hyponatermia and Transurethral Resection of Prostate Syndrome - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The keyed answer (hyperammonemia from hepatic/glycine-cleavage metabolism of absorbed glycine) is the single best answer and is the glycine-specific derangement that compounds dilutional hyponatremia in operative hysteroscopy intravascular absorption (OHIA)/TURP-type syndrome. All distractors are genuinely inferior: glycine absorption trends toward HYPOkalemia not hyperkalemia; glycine is not a clinically significant insulin secretagogue (no hypoglycemia); hypercalcemia is not a feature; glycine metabolites are ammonia/glyoxylate/glycolate, not methemoglobin-inducing. Stem is internally consistent (Na 119, deficit 2400 mL above the ~1000-1500 mL concern threshold, delayed somnolence/confusion, hypertension+bradycardia as a volume-overload/Cushing-type response). Rationales and management (stop procedure, free-water restriction, loop diuretic, reserve hypertonic saline for severe symptomatic hyponatremia with slow correction) are accurate and current. REFERENCE: The OpenAnesthesia URL resolves (loaded successfully, last updated Jan 2024) and is directly on-topic — it explicitly covers TURP syndrome extended to hysteroscopic procedures, names glycine 1.5%, and states large-volume glycine absorption causes hyperglycinemia, hyperammonemia (from glycine degradation), and transient visual disturbances. Strong citation match. Minor non-blocking note: the explanation mentions 'visual disturbance' in the clinical picture though the stem does not list visual symptoms, but this is consistent with glycine-toxicity literature and is not an error.

stem: A 64-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 38% predicted, baseline SpO2 92% on room air, uses home oxygen at night) is scheduled for an outpatient rotator cuff repair. The surgical team requests a single-shot interscalene brachial plexus block for the procedure and postoperative analgesia. After an uneventful ultrasound-guided block with 20 mL of 0.5% ropivacaine, the patient becomes progressively dyspneic and his SpO2 drops to 86%. Chest examination reveals decreased breath sounds and reduced movement at the right base. Which complication of this block is the MOST likely explanation?

choices.text: Ipsilateral phrenic nerve block causing hemidiaphragmatic paresis
choices.rationale: Correct: the interscalene approach blocks the ipsilateral phrenic nerve in nearly 100% of cases, producing hemidiaphragmatic paresis that is usually well tolerated but can precipitate respiratory compromise in a patient with limited pulmonary reserve such as severe COPD.

choices.text: Tension pneumothorax from pleural puncture
choices.rationale: Incorrect: pneumothorax is more associated with the supraclavicular approach and typically presents with absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, and hemodynamic instability rather than isolated basal hypoventilation.

choices.text: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity
choices.rationale: Incorrect: LAST presents with CNS excitation (metallic taste, seizures) and cardiac signs rather than an isolated unilateral diaphragmatic and respiratory picture.

choices.text: Recurrent laryngeal nerve block causing upper-airway obstruction
choices.rationale: Incorrect: recurrent laryngeal nerve block causes hoarseness and, if bilateral, airway obstruction with stridor, not unilateral basal hypoventilation and desaturation.

choices.text: High epidural or intrathecal spread of local anesthetic
choices.rationale: Incorrect: neuraxial spread would produce bilateral motor/sensory blockade and profound hypotension, which is not described here.

explanation: The interscalene block deposits local anesthetic where the phrenic nerve lies on the anterior scalene muscle, so ipsilateral phrenic nerve block and hemidiaphragmatic paresis occur in essentially all patients. Healthy patients tolerate the resulting ~25% drop in pulmonary function, but in patients with severe COPD or other marginal respiratory reserve it can cause clinically significant hypoxemia and dyspnea. For such patients, lower-volume techniques, more distal/diaphragm-sparing blocks, or an alternative analgesic plan should be considered. Ipsilateral Horner syndrome and recurrent laryngeal nerve block are other recognized side effects of this technique.
subtopic: Upper-extremity peripheral nerve block — interscalene block and hemidiaphragmatic paresis

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (ipsilateral phrenic nerve block causing hemidiaphragmatic paresis) is unambiguously the single best answer. Interscalene block produces ipsilateral hemidiaphragmatic paresis in ~100% of cases at standard volumes (20 mL 0.5% ropivacaine), with a ~25% reduction in pulmonary function — accurate, current figures. In a severe COPD patient with marginal reserve, this readily explains progressive dyspnea, desaturation to 86%, and unilateral basal reduced breath sounds/movement. All distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly explained: LAST presents with CNS/cardiac signs not isolated unilateral diaphragmatic findings; recurrent laryngeal nerve block causes hoarseness (airway obstruction only if bilateral); high neuraxial spread causes bilateral block plus hypotension; tension pneumothorax is rare with US-guided interscalene and would present with hemodynamic instability/tracheal deviation rather than this picture. The stem is internally consistent (right-sided block for a unilateral rotator cuff repair matches right basal findings). REFERENCE: WebFetch confirms https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/interscalene-block/ resolves (not a 404) and is squarely on-topic — it explicitly states the phrenic nerve lies on the anterior scalene and is routinely blocked, causing hemidiaphragmatic paralysis poorly tolerated in pulmonary disease, and lists Horner syndrome and other complications. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 29-year-old healthy woman receives a hyperbaric bupivacaine spinal anesthetic for an elective cesarean delivery. Shortly after positioning supine with left uterine displacement, her blood pressure falls from 122/74 to 78/40 mmHg and her heart rate drops from 88 to 52/min; she reports nausea and lightheadedness. She is breathing comfortably and is fully conscious. Which mechanism BEST explains this constellation of hypotension with bradycardia?

choices.text: Anaphylaxis to bupivacaine
choices.rationale: Incorrect: anaphylaxis would typically produce tachycardia, bronchospasm, urticaria, and airway/skin findings rather than isolated hypotension with bradycardia in a comfortable patient.

choices.text: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity from intravascular spread
choices.rationale: Incorrect: spinal doses are small and LAST presents with CNS excitation and cardiac dysrhythmias, not the orderly sympathectomy picture described here.

choices.text: Primary respiratory failure from a high block paralyzing the diaphragm
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the patient is breathing comfortably and conscious, so diaphragmatic (C3-C5) involvement is not the cause of her hemodynamic change.

choices.text: Sympathetic blockade reducing preload and afterload, with high-level block of the cardiac accelerator fibers
choices.rationale: Correct: neuraxial sympathectomy causes venodilation and arterial dilation (reduced preload and afterload), and when the block reaches the T1-T4 cardiac accelerator fibers it produces bradycardia, explaining hypotension with a slow heart rate.

choices.text: Hypovolemia from unrecognized surgical hemorrhage
choices.rationale: Incorrect: surgical hemorrhage before incision is implausible, and hypovolemia characteristically causes compensatory tachycardia rather than the bradycardia seen here.

explanation: Spinal anesthesia blocks sympathetic fibers that extend several dermatomes above the sensory level, producing venodilation and arterial dilation that drop preload and afterload and cause hypotension. When the block is high enough to reach the cardiac accelerator fibers (T1-T4), unopposed vagal tone produces bradycardia, so hypotension paired with bradycardia signals a high sympathectomy. Management includes left uterine displacement, fluids, and vasopressors (phenylephrine for hypotension; an agent with chronotropy such as ephedrine or atropine/epinephrine if bradycardia is prominent). Recognizing this physiology distinguishes an expected neuraxial effect from other causes of intraoperative hypotension.
subtopic: Neuraxial anesthesia — spinal-induced sympathectomy and high block physiology
reference_title: Spinal Anesthesia in Adults: Anatomy, Indications, and Physiological Effects — OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem is the textbook neuraxial sympathectomy presentation after a hyperbaric bupivacaine spinal for cesarean: hypotension WITH bradycardia in a comfortable, fully conscious patient. Keyed answer (index 3) — sympathetic blockade reducing preload/afterload plus high block reaching T1-T4 cardiac accelerator fibers causing bradycardia — is the single best and physiologically correct answer. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly refuted: anaphylaxis (would cause tachycardia/bronchospasm/urticaria, and bupivacaine anaphylaxis is rare), LAST (intrathecal not intravascular, small dose, presents with CNS excitation/arrhythmias), diaphragmatic high block (explicitly excluded by comfortable breathing and full consciousness), and hypovolemia/hemorrhage (pre-incision hemorrhage implausible, and hypovolemia causes compensatory tachycardia). The explanation's management (LUD, fluids, phenylephrine for hypotension, ephedrine/atropine/epinephrine when bradycardia prominent) is accurate and current. Stem deliberately closes off the respiratory and tachycardia alternatives, so no ambiguity. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/spinal-anesthesia/ resolves (not a 404) and is directly on-topic — it explicitly discusses autonomic blockade up to ~6 dermatomes above injection, reductions in preload/afterload, and bradycardia from unopposed parasympathetic tone when T1-T4 cardiac accelerator fibers are affected. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 68-year-old man is undergoing elective on-pump coronary artery bypass grafting. After successful weaning from cardiopulmonary bypass, the anesthesiologist-assistant begins administering protamine to reverse heparin. Approximately 90 seconds into the slow infusion, the central venous pressure and pulmonary artery pressures rise sharply (PA 58/34 mmHg from a baseline of 30/14), while the systemic arterial pressure falls to 62/38 mmHg. The surgeon notes the right ventricle is distended and poorly contractile. There is no airway pressure increase, no rash, and no bronchospasm. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?

choices.text: Stop the protamine infusion and support the circulation with a pulmonary vasodilator and inotropic/pressor support
choices.rationale: Correct — this presentation is a Type III catastrophic protamine reaction (acute pulmonary vasoconstriction with RV failure), and the priority is to halt the offending drug while supporting RV function and unloading the pulmonary circulation.

choices.text: Administer epinephrine and a large fluid bolus for presumed anaphylactic shock
choices.rationale: Incorrect — the absence of bronchospasm, urticaria, and elevated airway pressures argues against IgE-mediated anaphylaxis, and aggressive volume loading would further distend an already failing right ventricle.

choices.text: Continue the protamine and add phenylephrine to restore systemic pressure
choices.rationale: Incorrect — continuing the causative agent worsens the reaction, and pure alpha agonism raises pulmonary vascular resistance, aggravating the RV afterload mismatch.

choices.text: Give an additional bolus of heparin and prepare to resume cardiopulmonary bypass for hemorrhage control
choices.rationale: Incorrect — there is no evidence of bleeding, and the hemodynamic picture is one of acute pulmonary hypertension with RV failure, not inadequate anticoagulation.

choices.text: Increase the protamine infusion rate to complete reversal more quickly and shorten the exposure
choices.rationale: Incorrect — rapid protamine administration is itself a recognized trigger for hypotension and pulmonary vasoconstriction, so accelerating delivery would intensify the reaction.

explanation: Catastrophic (Type III) protamine reactions produce acute, often life-threatening pulmonary vasoconstriction with elevated PA pressures, systemic hypotension, and acute right ventricular failure, classically without the cutaneous or bronchospastic features of true anaphylaxis. Management is to stop the protamine immediately and support the circulation: reduce pulmonary vascular resistance (e.g., inhaled nitric oxide or other pulmonary vasodilators), support RV contractility with inotropes, and maintain systemic perfusion while avoiding agents and volume that worsen RV afterload or distension. Slow administration and vigilance during the first minutes of infusion are key preventive measures.
subtopic: Protamine reactions / separation from cardiopulmonary bypass

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem is a textbook Type III catastrophic protamine reaction — acute pulmonary vasoconstriction with elevated PA pressures, systemic hypotension, and acute RV failure/distension, with explicitly absent cutaneous and bronchospastic features that exclude IgE-mediated (Type I) anaphylaxis. Keyed answer (index 0: stop protamine, reduce PVR with a pulmonary vasodilator, support RV with inotrope/pressor) is the single best immediate management. Distractors are all genuinely inferior: (1) anaphylaxis misdiagnosis + large fluid bolus worsens an already distended failing RV; (2) continuing the offending agent plus pure alpha-agonism (phenylephrine) raises PVR/RV afterload; (3) no evidence of bleeding — note that reheparinization/return to CPB is a real rescue for REFRACTORY reactions, but the rationale given ('hemorrhage control') is the wrong indication and the patient is not described as refractory/arresting, so it is correctly keyed wrong; (4) faster protamine delivery is itself a recognized trigger. Explanation (inhaled NO/prostacyclin, inotropic RV support, avoid afterload/volume that worsen RV, slow administration as prevention) is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/protamine-reaction/ resolves (HTTP 200) and is directly on-topic — OpenAnesthesia 'Protamine Reactions' (Kim/Hollander, updated Jan 30 2025) explicitly covers Type III reactions with elevated PA pressures and RV failure, plus management (vasopressors, inotropes, pulmonary vasodilators, slow administration ≤20 mg/min). Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 54-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer presents to the preoperative area for an urgent subxiphoid pericardial window. She is dyspneic, sitting upright, with HR 124 bpm, BP 88/70 mmHg, and a narrow pulse pressure. JVP is markedly elevated and a 16-mmHg decrease in systolic pressure is noted during inspiration. Bedside echocardiography shows a large pericardial effusion with right atrial systolic collapse and right ventricular early-diastolic collapse. Which of the following best describes the appropriate hemodynamic strategy for inducing anesthesia in this patient?

choices.text: Deepen anesthesia first with a high-dose propofol induction to blunt the sympathetic response before incision
choices.rationale: Incorrect — propofol-induced vasodilation and loss of sympathetic tone can precipitate abrupt cardiovascular collapse in a preload-dependent, tamponade-physiology patient.

choices.text: Maintain a relatively fast heart rate, adequate preload, and high systemic vascular resistance, ideally prepping and draining under local before full induction
choices.rationale: Correct — tamponade physiology demands keeping the patient 'fast, full, and tight,' and preparing/draining the effusion before committing to general anesthesia and positive-pressure ventilation minimizes the risk of collapse.

choices.text: Administer a beta-blocker to control the tachycardia and reduce myocardial oxygen demand
choices.rationale: Incorrect — the tachycardia is a compensatory mechanism to maintain cardiac output in the face of a fixed, reduced stroke volume, and blunting it can cause decompensation.

choices.text: Give a generous dose of nitroglycerin to reduce preload and relieve the congestion suggested by the elevated JVP
choices.rationale: Incorrect — the elevated JVP reflects impaired filling from external compression, not volume overload, and reducing preload would worsen the obstructive shock.

choices.text: Initiate positive-pressure ventilation immediately after a rapid-sequence induction to optimize oxygenation before drainage
choices.rationale: Incorrect — positive-pressure ventilation raises intrathoracic pressure, decreases venous return, and can precipitate cardiovascular collapse before the pericardium is decompressed.

explanation: Cardiac tamponade is an obstructive shock state in which elevated intrapericardial pressure impairs diastolic filling, producing tachycardia, hypotension, narrow pulse pressure, elevated JVP, and pulsus paradoxus, with echocardiographic right atrial and right ventricular collapse. The hemodynamic goals are 'fast, full, and tight': preserve heart rate, maintain preload, and support systemic vascular resistance. Because induction abolishes compensatory sympathetic tone and positive-pressure ventilation further reduces venous return, the safest approach is to prep and drain the effusion under local anesthesia whenever feasible, reserving general anesthesia for after decompression.
subtopic: Pericardial disease / cardiac tamponade physiology

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem is an internally consistent, textbook presentation of cardiac tamponade (malignant effusion, tachycardia, narrow pulse pressure, elevated JVP, pulsus paradoxus of 16 mmHg, plus correctly assigned echo findings — RA systolic collapse and RV early-diastolic collapse). The keyed answer (index 1) reflects the standard "fast, full, and tight" goals plus draining under local before committing to GA/PPV, which is the accepted approach and matches the cited reference verbatim. All four distractors describe genuinely harmful interventions in tamponade physiology: high-dose propofol (vasodilation/sympatholysis → collapse), beta-blockade (removes compensatory tachycardia for a fixed stroke volume), nitroglycerin (preload reduction worsens obstructive shock), and immediate PPV after RSI (decreased venous return before decompression). Rationales are accurate and current; no second defensible answer. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/cardiac-tamponade/ resolves successfully and is directly on-topic — it explicitly covers tamponade physiology, the "Fast, Full, and Tight" hemodynamic goals, avoidance of induction/PPV when feasible, pulsus paradoxus, and the RA/RV collapse echo findings used in the stem. Both dimensions sound.

stem: A 72-year-old man with a dual-chamber permanent pacemaker (implanted for symptomatic sinus node dysfunction; he is pacemaker-dependent) is scheduled for an open umbilical hernia repair under general anesthesia. The surgeon plans to use a monopolar electrosurgical unit. The device interrogation report indicates the pacemaker is programmed DDD at a lower rate of 60 bpm. Which of the following is the most appropriate intraoperative strategy to manage the risk of electromagnetic interference?

choices.text: Apply a magnet over the device and leave it in place for the entire case to permanently disable all sensing
choices.rationale: Incorrect — a magnet typically produces asynchronous pacing only while in place and its effect is device- and manufacturer-specific, so it is neither a permanent nor universally reliable solution and must be tested/confirmed.

choices.text: Have the device reprogrammed to an asynchronous mode (e.g., DOO) preoperatively or place a magnet to achieve asynchronous pacing, and position the electrosurgical return pad so current flows away from the generator
choices.rationale: Correct — in a pacemaker-dependent patient, converting to asynchronous pacing prevents EMI-induced inhibition, and orienting the return-electrode pad so the current path avoids the generator/leads minimizes interference.

choices.text: Proceed with the case in DDD mode and rely on the device's built-in noise-rejection to prevent any pacing inhibition
choices.rationale: Incorrect — monopolar electrosurgery is the most common source of EMI and can be oversensed as intrinsic activity, inhibiting pacing output and causing dangerous bradycardia or asystole in a dependent patient.

choices.text: Cancel the procedure until the pacemaker can be removed and replaced with a leadless device
choices.rationale: Incorrect — EMI risk during surgery is routinely managed with reprogramming or magnet use and appropriate cautery technique, so device explantation is unnecessary and inappropriate.

choices.text: Switch the surgeon to using long, continuous bursts of monopolar cautery to reduce the total number of EMI events
choices.rationale: Incorrect — short, intermittent cautery bursts (and bipolar when possible) are recommended to limit EMI exposure, whereas long continuous activation increases the duration of interference.

explanation: Monopolar electrosurgery is the most common source of electromagnetic interference in the operating room and can be oversensed by a pacemaker, inhibiting output and causing bradycardia or asystole in a pacemaker-dependent patient. The two principal mitigation strategies are to render pacing asynchronous (preoperative reprogramming to an asynchronous mode such as DOO, or intraoperative magnet application after confirming the device's magnet response) and to optimize cautery technique—using bipolar when possible, short intermittent bursts, and positioning the return-electrode pad so the current path is directed away from the generator and leads. Magnet effects vary by manufacturer and are temporary, so they must be verified rather than assumed.
subtopic: Cardiac implantable electronic devices / perioperative EMI management
reference_title: OpenAnesthesia — Cardiac Implantable Electronic Devices: Pacemakers

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. For a pacemaker-dependent patient (DDD 60) undergoing surgery with a monopolar ESU, the keyed answer (index 1) — render pacing asynchronous via preoperative DOO reprogramming OR magnet (after confirming magnet response), plus position the return pad so current flows away from the generator/leads — is the single best answer and matches ASA/HRS consensus. All four distractors carry genuine errors: (0) magnet effect is temporary and device-specific, not a permanent disabling of sensing; (2) relying on DDD noise-rejection risks oversensing/inhibition and asystole; (3) explant/leadless replacement is inappropriate; (4) long continuous cautery bursts increase EMI exposure (short intermittent bursts are preferred). Options 0 and 1 both invoke a magnet but do not create ambiguity, because option 0 is defeated by its false 'permanent/disable all sensing' claim. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves (not 404); page is titled 'Cardiac Implantable Electronic Devices: Pacemakers' and directly covers EMI from the electrosurgery unit, magnet/asynchronous pacing, pacemaker dependency, and placing the dispersing pad so ESU current does not cross the generator — fully on-topic. Both checks pass.

stem: A 4-year-old, 16-kg child is undergoing strabismus repair under general endotracheal anesthesia with sevoflurane in oxygen/air, mechanically ventilated. Roughly 40 minutes into a stable case, the capnograph waveform — which had been showing a normal square pattern with EtCO2 of 38 mmHg — gradually loses its flat alveolar plateau and develops an upsloping phase III, while the EtCO2 rises to 49 mmHg over several minutes. Peak inspiratory pressure has crept up by 6 cmH2O, and on auscultation there are bilateral expiratory wheezes. Heart rate and SpO2 are unchanged. Which of the following is the MOST likely explanation for this capnographic change?

choices.text: An esophageal intubation that was initially undetected
choices.rationale: Esophageal intubation produces little or no sustained CO2 and a rapidly decaying or absent waveform, not a gradually upsloping plateau with maintained EtCO2.

choices.text: Acute pulmonary embolism with increased dead space
choices.rationale: Significant embolism increases alveolar dead space and characteristically lowers EtCO2 with a widened arterial-to-EtCO2 gradient, not a rising EtCO2 with an upsloping plateau.

choices.text: Exhausted CO2 absorbent allowing rebreathing
choices.rationale: Absorbent exhaustion elevates the inspired CO2 baseline (the waveform fails to return to zero) rather than producing a selectively upsloping expiratory phase III with new wheezing.

choices.text: Bronchospasm causing uneven alveolar emptying
choices.rationale: Lower-airway obstruction prolongs and staggers alveolar emptying, producing the classic upsloping ('shark-fin') phase III, rising EtCO2, increased airway pressure, and wheezing seen here.

choices.text: Hypoventilation from a leak around an uncuffed tube
choices.rationale: A circuit or tube leak lowers tidal volume and EtCO2 and may blunt the plateau height, but it does not create progressive upsloping with wheezing and rising airway pressures.

explanation: An upsloping (sloped, 'shark-fin') phase III reflects asynchronous, prolonged alveolar emptying from increased expiratory airway resistance, the hallmark of bronchospasm or other lower-airway obstruction. The accompanying rise in peak inspiratory pressure, new expiratory wheeze, and gradually climbing EtCO2 (from impaired ventilation) all point to bronchospasm rather than a dead-space or rebreathing problem. Loss of the normal flat plateau is the key waveform clue that the abnormality is in airway emptying mechanics, and treatment is deepening anesthesia and beta-2 agonists.
subtopic: Capnography waveform interpretation

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer D (bronchospasm) is the single best, unambiguous answer. The vignette is a textbook lower-airway obstruction picture: gradual loss of the flat alveolar plateau with an upsloping ('shark-fin') phase III from asynchronous/prolonged alveolar emptying due to increased expiratory resistance, plus corroborating new bilateral expiratory wheezes, rising peak inspiratory pressure (+6 cmH2O), and a climbing EtCO2 (38->49) from impaired ventilation. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: (A) esophageal intubation gives an absent/rapidly-decaying waveform, not a sustained upsloping plateau, and would not appear 40 min into a stable case; (B) pulmonary embolism increases alveolar dead space and characteristically LOWERS EtCO2 with a widened a-ET gradient (opposite direction); (C) exhausted absorbent raises the inspired CO2 baseline so the waveform fails to return to zero, a distinct feature, without selective phase III upslope or wheezing; (E) a tube/circuit leak lowers tidal volume and EtCO2 and does not produce upslope with rising PIP and wheezing. The explanation and management (deepen anesthesia + beta-2 agonists) are accurate and current. The only theoretical confounder for an upsloping phase III (COPD) is irrelevant in a 4-year-old; the acute onset with new wheeze and rising PIP cleanly isolates bronchospasm. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK539754/ resolves to 'Capnography and Respiratory Monitoring' (StatPearls, NCBI Bookshelf), which covers capnograph waveform phases including phase III, EtCO2 interpretation, and hypo/hyperventilation patterns. It is genuinely on-topic for capnography waveform interpretation. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 23-year-old man is brought from a house fire to the OR for urgent fasciotomy of a circumferentially burned forearm. He is intubated and hemodynamically stable. The pulse oximeter reads SpO2 98% on 100% oxygen, yet a co-oximetry panel from an arterial sample reports carboxyhemoglobin of 22% with a measured (functional) oxyhemoglobin saturation of 76%. Which statement BEST explains the discrepancy between the bedside pulse oximeter and the co-oximeter?

choices.text: Carboxyhemoglobin absorbs light almost identically to oxyhemoglobin at 660 nm, so a two-wavelength pulse oximeter reads it as if it were oxygenated
choices.rationale: Conventional two-wavelength pulse oximetry cannot distinguish carboxyhemoglobin from oxyhemoglobin because their absorbance at 660 nm is nearly the same, so a high COHb falsely elevates the displayed SpO2.

choices.text: Carboxyhemoglobin absorbs maximally at 940 nm, driving the calculated ratio toward a falsely low reading
choices.rationale: This is incorrect: carboxyhemoglobin does not peak at 940 nm, and the clinical error from COHb is a falsely HIGH SpO2, not a low one.

choices.text: The pulse oximeter is reading venous rather than arterial pulsations because of the burn
choices.rationale: Pulse oximeters isolate the pulsatile (arterial) component of absorbance, and venous sampling is not the mechanism of the COHb discrepancy.

choices.text: Methemoglobin, not carboxyhemoglobin, is the cause and forces the SpO2 toward 85%
choices.rationale: The panel specifies carboxyhemoglobin, and methemoglobin (which drives SpO2 toward 85%) is a different dyshemoglobin not implicated here.

choices.text: Profound anemia from blood loss has lowered the signal-to-noise ratio and falsely raised the reading
choices.rationale: Anemia affects the amount of hemoglobin but pulse oximetry remains accurate over a wide hematocrit range, and anemia is not the source of this COHb-driven error.

explanation: Standard pulse oximeters use only two wavelengths (660 and 940 nm) and assume blood contains only oxyhemoglobin and deoxyhemoglobin. Carboxyhemoglobin has an absorption spectrum at 660 nm very close to that of oxyhemoglobin, so it is counted as 'oxygenated,' producing a falsely normal or high SpO2 despite dangerously low true oxygen-carrying saturation. Only multi-wavelength CO-oximetry (or a pulse CO-oximeter) can quantify carboxyhemoglobin, which is why co-oximetry is essential in suspected carbon monoxide poisoning.
subtopic: Pulse oximetry physics and dyshemoglobin limitations

verdict.reason: Clinical: Keyed answer (index 0) is the single best answer. Standard two-wavelength pulse oximetry (660/940 nm) cannot distinguish carboxyhemoglobin from oxyhemoglobin because COHb absorbance at 660 nm is nearly identical to O2Hb, so high COHb is counted as oxygenated and falsely elevates SpO2 — matching the stem (SpO2 98% but functional saturation 76% with COHb 22%). This is classic, current teaching (Barker). All distractors are genuinely inferior: index 1 has wrong peak wavelength and wrong error direction (falsely high, not low); index 2's venous-pulsation mechanism is wrong (oximeters isolate the arterial pulsatile component); index 3 invokes methemoglobin (~85% pull) which the panel does not show; index 4's anemia does not cause this error and oximetry is accurate over a wide hematocrit range. Rationales and explanation are accurate. The 'almost identically' phrasing is a mild simplification (absorbances are similar, not literally identical) but does not create ambiguity. Reference: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470348/ resolves to StatPearls 'Pulse Oximetry', which explicitly covers 660/940 nm physics, dyshemoglobinemia (carboxyhemoglobinemia/methemoglobinemia) causing false readings, and the recommendation to confirm with a co-oximeter. Directly on-topic. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: An 81-year-old man with mild baseline cognitive impairment, hypertension, and benign prostatic hyperplasia undergoes open reduction and internal fixation of a hip fracture under general anesthesia. The case is uncomplicated. Preoperatively he received midazolam 2 mg IV for anxiety; intraoperatively he received fentanyl, sevoflurane, and a dose of scopolamine for presumed PONV prophylaxis. On postoperative day 1 he is inattentive, disoriented, and has a fluctuating level of consciousness with intermittent agitation; vital signs, glucose, oxygen saturation, and a basic metabolic panel are all within normal limits. Which element of his perioperative management most likely contributed to this presentation and should be avoided in similar patients?

choices.text: Administration of preoperative midazolam and intraoperative scopolamine
choices.rationale: Correct: benzodiazepines and anticholinergics that cross the blood-brain barrier (such as scopolamine) are well-established precipitating drugs for postoperative delirium in elderly patients and should be avoided.

choices.text: Use of sevoflurane as the volatile maintenance agent
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while deep anesthesia should be avoided, sevoflurane itself is not a specific delirium precipitant comparable to benzodiazepines and anticholinergics, and no agent-specific harm is implicated here.

choices.text: Choice of a general rather than neuraxial anesthetic technique
choices.rationale: Incorrect: large trials have not shown that general versus neuraxial anesthesia meaningfully changes postoperative delirium incidence, so technique is not the best answer.

choices.text: Intraoperative use of fentanyl for analgesia
choices.rationale: Incorrect: short-acting opioids like fentanyl are far less deliriogenic than meperidine, and undertreated pain (not fentanyl) is the larger delirium risk.

choices.text: The hip fracture surgery itself in an octogenarian
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although hip surgery and advanced age are nonmodifiable risk factors, the question asks for a modifiable management element that should be avoided.

explanation: Postoperative delirium is an acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and cognition that is common in older surgical patients, particularly after hip fracture repair. Benzodiazepines (e.g., midazolam) and CNS-penetrating anticholinergics (e.g., scopolamine, as well as meperidine) are among the strongest modifiable pharmacologic precipitants and should be avoided in at-risk geriatric patients. Management emphasizes removing offending drugs, treating reversible contributors, and nonpharmacologic measures such as reorientation and early mobilization.
subtopic: Postoperative neurocognitive disorders / delirium prevention

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem describes classic hyperactive postoperative delirium on POD1 in an 81-year-old after hip fracture ORIF, with reversible causes (hypoxia, hypoglycemia, metabolic, hemodynamic) explicitly excluded. The keyed answer (preop midazolam + intraoperative scopolamine) is the single best choice: benzodiazepines and CNS-penetrating anticholinergics — scopolamine is a tertiary amine that crosses the blood-brain barrier — are among the strongest MODIFIABLE pharmacologic precipitants of POD, consistent with ASA/AGS Best Practice and ESAIC POD guidelines. The stem deliberately bundles two distinct high-risk deliriogenic drugs, cleanly separating it from the distractors. All distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate/current: sevoflurane is not an agent-specific delirium precipitant; large RCTs/meta-analyses (e.g., RAGA) show no meaningful delirium difference between general vs neuraxial; short-acting fentanyl is far less deliriogenic than meperidine and undertreated pain is the larger risk; and hip surgery/age are nonmodifiable while the stem explicitly asks for a modifiable element. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK534831/ resolves to StatPearls 'Postoperative Delirium,' which is directly on-topic and explicitly lists benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, and meperidine as deliriogenic drugs to avoid — matching the keyed answer and explanation. Both checks pass.

stem: A 79-year-old woman with a serum creatinine of 1.8 mg/dL (eGFR 32 mL/min/1.73m^2) and a history of cirrhosis presents for laparoscopic cholecystectomy. She has decreased lean body mass and a reduced cardiac output. You plan deep neuromuscular blockade with reliable, predictable recovery at the end of a relatively short case, and you are concerned about residual paralysis in the PACU. Which neuromuscular blocking agent's duration of action is LEAST affected by this patient's age-related and organ-related changes?

choices.text: Cisatracurium
choices.rationale: Correct: cisatracurium is eliminated predominantly by organ-independent Hofmann elimination, so its duration is not prolonged by the hepatic and renal impairment that accompany aging.

choices.text: Vecuronium
choices.rationale: Incorrect: vecuronium depends on hepatic and renal elimination, so its action is prolonged in elderly patients with hepatic or renal impairment.

choices.text: Rocuronium
choices.rationale: Incorrect: rocuronium is primarily hepatically (biliary) eliminated with some renal contribution, and reduced hepatic blood flow in the elderly prolongs its effect.

choices.text: Pancuronium
choices.rationale: Incorrect: pancuronium is a long-acting agent heavily dependent on renal elimination, making it a poor choice when predictable short recovery is desired in renal impairment.

choices.text: Succinylcholine
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although metabolized by plasma cholinesterase, succinylcholine is a depolarizing agent unsuitable for maintaining deep nondepolarizing-type blockade and is not the comparison being asked about for prolonged nondepolarizing recovery.

explanation: Aging reduces hepatic and renal blood flow and function, prolonging the action of nondepolarizing neuromuscular blockers that depend on organ-based clearance (vecuronium, rocuronium, pancuronium). Cisatracurium undergoes Hofmann elimination, a spontaneous temperature- and pH-dependent degradation that is independent of hepatic and renal function, giving it a predictable recovery profile in patients with organ impairment. Residual blockade is more frequent in elderly patients, so quantitative neuromuscular monitoring remains essential regardless of agent choice.
subtopic: Geriatric pharmacology — neuromuscular blocking agents
reference_title: Pharmacological Changes with Aging: Opioids and Neuromuscular Blocking Agents — OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Cisatracurium is eliminated predominantly by organ-independent Hofmann elimination (spontaneous temperature/pH-dependent degradation), so its duration is not prolonged by the renal/hepatic impairment of aging — the single best answer.
